text: The robotic sleeve gastrectomy begins with the surgeon measuring six centimeters proximal to the pylorus and marking this area with a suture. The large liver is visible, with a focus on handling the wet lobe during the procedure.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac and begins taking down the greater omentum along the greater curvature of the stomach, moving towards the pylorus. The duodenum is visible posterior to the stomach. The dissection proceeds in the direction of the short gastric vessels and the spleen.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the large left lobe of the liver to bring the anatomical structures closer into view for dissection, with a bougie visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon assesses the visualization of the surgical field and decides to mobilize the liver for better access. A small capsular tear on the liver is observed and controlled by applying pressure.

The surgeon attempts to retract the proximal part of the left lobe of the fatty liver by using a suture to elevate it like a canopy. Due to concerns about capsule tearing, gauze is applied to distribute the force evenly. One end of the suture is attached to the Nathanson retractor, while the other end is placed through the falciform ligament.
Using the robotic platform, the surgeon manipulates and places sutures on the interior aspect of the abdomen. The diaphragm is visualized, and dissection progresses towards the left crura.
The spleen is visualized as the dissection progresses from the posterior aspect, with attention to maintaining awareness of the location of the left gastric vasculature.
The surgeon carefully dissects the filmy tissue along the lesser curvature of the stomach, taking care to avoid injury to the visible blood vessels in this area.

The surgeon has completely mobilized the cardiac region and identifies the left crura. The previously used suture is removed, and the vessels along the lesser curvature are observed with excellent pulsation.

The liver is retracted using the robotic arm number three, with gauze placed to prevent injury to the liver capsule. The surgeon prepares for the first firing with a black load, ensuring careful positioning to avoid impinging on the incisor.

The surgeon visualizes both the anterior and posterior elbows of the stomach, preparing for imprecation of the staple line. A large lipoma is discovered along the gastroesophageal junction, and the surgeon decides to partially remove it for better visualization of the angle of His.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the redundant portion of the posterior fundus and cardia to ensure it remains with the specimen, avoiding accidental thermal injury to the underlying stomach tissue.
The specimen is moved aside, and the surgeon begins to imbricate the staple line using the Lambert technique, avoiding direct suturing through the staple line.

While it may take a little bit longer to over-sew the staple line, one potentially saves on the cost of utilizing the buttressing material. And while there's no definitive answer as to the best way to reinforce the staple line, this is just one of the few good options that are available.

The surgeon performs a methylene blue test, which returns negative, indicating no leakage. There is no visible bleeding from the previous capsular tear. Biological fibrin glue is applied to the area, and additional staples are used to address increased oozing.
